title: 1 Corinthians 7
subtitle: Marriage, Singleness, and the Present Crisis

sections.header: Verses 1–16 — "Each Has Their Own Gift from God"

heb.paragraph: The noun charisma (gift) in v. 7 applies the language of spiritual gifts to marital status. Both marriage and celibacy are charismata — grace-gifts from God, not achievements or obligations. This framing prevents both the elevation of celibacy as inherently superior (an ascetic tendency) and the dismissal of singleness as deficient (a social convention). Paul thus relativises marital status: each person has received their own charisma from God.

refs.note: The creation narrative establishes marriage as God’s provision for companionship and the one-flesh union that forms the background to Paul’s teaching.
Jesus’ teaching on marriage, divorce, and the call to celibacy for the sake of the kingdom provides the dominical context for Paul’s discussion.
The prophetic tradition treating marriage as a covenant parallels Paul’s emphasis on mutual obligation.

notes.note: The phrase ‘it is good for a man not to have sexual relations’ is likely a Corinthian ascetic slogan, not Paul’s thesis. Paul’s correction: ‘since sexual immorality is occurring’ (dia de tas porneias), marriage is God’s provision for sexual expression. The phrase does not reduce marriage to a remedy for lust but recognises the reality of human sexuality.

Paul’s instruction on conjugal rights is remarkably egalitarian for the first century: the wife’s body belongs to the husband, and the husband’s body belongs to the wife. Neither partner may unilaterally withhold conjugal relations. Temporary abstinence is permitted only by mutual consent, for prayer, and for a limited time.

Paul wishes all were celibate like himself, but he recognises that celibacy is a gift (charisma) not given to all. Those who cannot exercise self-control should marry: ‘it is better to marry than to burn with passion.’ This is not a depreciation of marriage but a realistic acknowledgment of diverse callings.

Paul distinguishes between the Lord’s command (vv. 10–11, from Jesus’ teaching on divorce) and his own apostolic instruction (vv. 12–16, addressing mixed marriages not covered by Jesus’ teaching). A believing spouse should not divorce an unbelieving partner who is willing to stay. The unbelieving spouse is ‘sanctified’ through the believer — not saved, but set within the sphere of holiness that the believing partner brings to the household.

Calvin strongly opposes any reading that elevates celibacy above marriage. He notes that Paul’s instruction on mutual conjugal obligation undermines both the Roman Catholic requirement of clerical celibacy and the Corinthian ascetic tendency to withhold marital intimacy.

Calvin distinguishes two situations: (1) marriages between believers, where Jesus’ prohibition of divorce applies; (2) mixed marriages, where Paul gives apostolic guidance. Calvin stresses that desertion by an unbelieving spouse constitutes a legitimate ground for dissolving the marriage: the believer ‘is not bound’ (ou dedoulotai).

The phrase kalon anthrōpō gynaikos mē haptesthai (‘it is good for a man not to touch a woman’) is almost certainly a quotation from the Corinthians’ letter. The verb haptesthai (‘to touch’) is a euphemism for sexual relations.

The verb hēgiastai (‘has been sanctified’) in this context does not mean ‘saved’ but ‘set apart’ within the sphere of holiness that characterises the believing household. The children are similarly ‘holy’ (hagia) in this relational sense.

Fee argues that Paul’s personal preference for celibacy reflects his eschatological urgency (‘the time is short,’ v. 29) rather than any ascetic devaluation of the body. Marriage is a full charisma, not a concession. Paul’s mutuality language (vv. 3–4) is revolutionary: the wife has authority (exousia) over the husband’s body, an extraordinary claim in a patriarchal culture.

Fee notes that Paul carefully distinguishes the Lord’s command (divorce prohibition, vv. 10–11) from his own apostolic judgment (mixed marriages, vv. 12–16). This distinction does not diminish Paul’s authority but demonstrates his integrity: he will not attribute to Jesus what he knows comes from his own Spirit-guided reflection.

Thiselton argues that the mutuality of conjugal obligation in vv. 3–4 represents a radical departure from both Jewish and Greco-Roman patriarchal norms. The wife’s authority over the husband’s body is without parallel in ancient literature and reflects Paul’s consistent egalitarianism in Christ.

Thiselton draws attention to Paul’s hermeneutical self-awareness: the distinction between ‘the Lord, not I’ (v. 10) and ‘I, not the Lord’ (v. 12) shows Paul operating with a developed sense of the relationship between dominical tradition and apostolic authority.

hist.context: Paul now responds to questions raised in the Corinthians’ letter (‘now for the matters you wrote about,’ v. 1). The phrase peri de (‘now concerning’) marks a shift to a new topic and recurs at 7:25; 8:1; 12:1; 16:1, 12. The Corinthians’ statement ‘it is good for a man not to have sexual relations with a woman’ (v. 1b) is likely a slogan from an ascetic faction within the church, not Paul’s own view. Some Corinthians, perhaps influenced by Cynic or proto-Gnostic ideas about the body’s irrelevance, were apparently advocating abstinence within marriage. Paul’s response is nuanced: he values celibacy (v. 7) but insists that marriage involves mutual obligation (vv. 3–5) and that sexual abstinence within marriage must be temporary and consensual.

sections.header: Verses 17–24 — "Remain in the Situation God Called You To"

heb.paragraph: The noun klēsis (calling) in vv. 17–24 undergoes a distinctive semantic shift. In standard Pauline usage, klēsis refers to God’s saving call (cf. 1:26; Rom 11:29). Here Paul extends the concept: the social situation in which a person was called (married/unmarried, circumcised/uncircumcised, slave/free) becomes itself a legitimate sphere for living out the divine call. Klēsis thus bridges soteriology and social ethics: the circumstances of conversion are not obstacles to faithfulness but arenas for it.

refs.note: Paul’s declaration that in Christ there is neither Jew nor Gentile, slave nor free, male nor female provides the theological complement to the social realism of 1 Cor 7.
Paul’s letter to Philemon addresses the concrete case of a slave (Onesimus), suggesting the social implications of the gospel without mandating social revolution.
Paul’s household code addresses slaves and masters within the framework of serving Christ.

notes.note: The principle ‘remain as you were called’ applies first to circumcision. Circumcision is nothing; uncircumcision is nothing. What matters is keeping God’s commands. This radical relativisation of the law’s boundary markers echoes Galatians 5:6 and 6:15.

The application to slavery is pastorally sensitive. Paul does not command slaves to seek freedom, but neither does he prohibit it: ‘if you can gain your freedom, do so’ (v. 21b, though the Greek is debated). The governing truth is that the slave called by the Lord is the Lord’s freedperson, and the free person called is Christ’s slave. All social categories are relativised by union with Christ.

Calvin reads the principle of remaining in one’s calling as an affirmation of ordinary vocations: God does not require Christians to abandon their occupations or social positions but to sanctify them. This teaching becomes foundational for the Reformation doctrine of vocation.

Calvin interprets v. 21 as encouraging slaves to use the opportunity for freedom if it arises, while insisting that servitude does not diminish one’s standing before God. The purchase metaphor (‘you were bought at a price,’ v. 23) means believers belong to Christ alone, making spiritual slavery to human masters impossible.

The phrase mallon chrēsai (‘rather use’ or ‘rather avail yourself’) is notoriously ambiguous: does Paul mean ‘make use of the opportunity for freedom’ or ‘make use of your present condition as a slave’? The context (vv. 22–23) slightly favours the former, but certainty is impossible.

The phrase apeleutheros kyriou (‘the Lord’s freedperson’) and doulos Christou (‘Christ’s slave’) create a paradox: every Christian is simultaneously free (from sin) and enslaved (to Christ), regardless of social status.

Fee argues that the threefold repetition of the principle (‘remain as you are’ in vv. 17, 20, 24) structures the entire chapter and reflects Paul’s eschatological conviction: because the present form of the world is passing away (v. 31), social rearrangement is not a spiritual priority. This is not social conservatism but eschatological pragmatism.

Fee reads v. 21 as advising slaves to take freedom if offered, consistent with Paul’s general stance that freedom is preferable to slavery. But the theological point transcends the practical: in Christ, the slave is already free and the free person already Christ’s slave.

Thiselton emphasises that Paul’s principle is not quietism but a sophisticated theological claim: the call of God transforms every social situation into a venue for faithful witness. The circumstances of calling become the arena, not the obstacle, for discipleship.

Thiselton surveys the extensive scholarly debate on v. 21 and concludes that the ambiguity is deliberate: Paul’s primary concern is not social policy but the theological truth that Christ’s purchase relativises all human claims to ownership.

hist.context: Paul articulates a general principle that governs not just marriage but all social circumstances: each person should remain in the situation they were in when God called them (v. 17). He applies this to circumcision (vv. 18–19) and slavery (vv. 21–23), then restates the principle (v. 24). The principle is not a blanket endorsement of the status quo but a theological statement that salvation in Christ does not require social revolution as a precondition for faithful living. In the context of the letter’s eschatological urgency (‘the time is short,’ v. 29), rearranging one’s social circumstances is less important than living faithfully within them.

heb.paragraph: The adjective amerimnos (free from anxiety) in v. 32 captures Paul’s primary pastoral motive for commending singleness. The unmarried person is able to be amerimnos — undistracted in devotion to the Lord. Paul is not opposing marriage but acknowledging that marriage inevitably introduces divided attention (merimnaō, ‘to be anxious/concerned,’ vv. 32–34). His goal is the Corinthians’ benefit (to hymōn autōn symphoron, v. 35), not to impose restrictions.

refs.note: Jesus’ warning that ‘how dreadful it will be in those days for pregnant women and nursing mothers’ reflects the same eschatological urgency about family responsibilities during tribulation.
Paul’s teaching on the Lord’s return and the gathering of believers provides the eschatological framework for the urgency expressed here.
Paul’s call to ‘wake up from slumber’ because ‘the night is nearly over’ shares the temporal compression of 1 Cor 7:29–31.

notes.note: Paul gives his opinion (gnōmē), not a dominical command, recommending that virgins remain as they are because of ‘the present crisis.’ Those who marry do not sin, but they will face trouble (thlipsis) in this life. Paul’s advice is pastoral, not dogmatic: he wants to spare them unnecessary hardship.

The seven ‘as if not’ (hōs mē) clauses constitute one of the most radical statements in the NT: those who have wives should live as if they do not; those who mourn, as if they do not; those who are happy, as if they are not; those who buy, as if they do not possess; those who use the things of the world, as if not engrossed in them. This is eschatological detachment: all present realities are relativised by the coming transformation.

Paul’s concern is ‘undivided devotion to the Lord’ (v. 35). The unmarried person can give undistracted attention to the Lord’s affairs; the married person is inevitably divided between pleasing the Lord and pleasing the spouse. This is not a depreciation of marriage but a recognition of competing loyalties.

Calvin notes Paul’s honesty: he does not fabricate a dominical command but gives his own trustworthy judgment. Calvin applies this to pastoral counsel generally: where Scripture is silent, the pastor may advise but must not legislate.

Calvin interprets the hōs mē clauses as a call to hold all earthly goods with a loose grip. The world’s form (schēma) is passing away; only what belongs to God’s kingdom endures. Calvin cautions that this does not mean indifference to earthly duties but a proper ordering of priorities.

The phrase tēn enestōsan anankēn (‘the present crisis’ or ‘the impending distress’) is debated: does enestōsan mean ‘present’ (already here) or ‘impending’ (about to come)? In Pauline usage it typically means ‘present’ (Rom 8:38; Gal 1:4).

The phrase paragi to schēma tou kosmou toutou (‘the present form of this world is passing away’) uses schēma (‘outward form/configuration’) rather than ousia (‘substance’). The world’s appearance and structure are transient, not its underlying reality.

Fee argues that the ‘present crisis’ is not a local famine but the eschatological tribulation that Paul believed was already underway. The hōs mē clauses express an eschatological existence: believers live in the overlap of the ages, present in the world but oriented toward the coming kingdom.

Fee stresses that Paul’s argument is not about the superiority of celibacy per se but about its practical advantage in a time of eschatological urgency. Marriage is not sinful but distracting; celibacy is not holier but more strategically useful.

Thiselton identifies the hōs mē clauses as an example of Paul’s ‘eschatological reservation’: believers participate fully in the world’s structures (marriage, commerce, grief, joy) while recognising their penultimate character. This is not Stoic detachment but christological reorientation.

Thiselton notes that Paul’s concern for ‘undivided devotion’ (euparedron, v. 35) uses a term that in secular usage meant ‘sitting beside’ in attentive service. The image is of undistracted attendance on the Lord.

hist.context: Paul introduces another topic from the Corinthians’ letter with peri de (‘now about,’ v. 25): the question of virgins (parthenoi). The term may refer to unmarried women, betrothed women, or both. Paul acknowledges he has no direct command from the Lord (v. 25) but offers his judgment as one who is trustworthy (pistos) by the Lord’s mercy. His advice is shaped by ‘the present crisis’ (tēn enestōsan anankēn, v. 26), which may refer to an immediate famine or social upheaval, or more likely to the eschatological tribulation that Paul expected to intensify before Christ’s return. The compressed timeframe (‘the time is short,’ ho kairos synestalmenos estin, v. 29) relativises all earthly commitments.

sections.header: Verses 36–40 — "He Who Marries Does Right; He Who Does Not, Does Better"

heb.paragraph: The verb gamizō (to give in marriage) in vv. 36–38 is interpreted in three main ways: (1) a father deciding whether to give his virgin daughter in marriage; (2) a man deciding whether to marry his betrothed; (3) a ‘spiritual marriage’ (virgines subintroductae) where couples lived together in celibate partnership. Most modern scholars favour option (2), reading the passage as advice to a man about whether to marry his fiancée. The cultural context of arranged marriage in the Greco-Roman world makes option (1) plausible, but the personal language of vv. 36–37 suggests the man’s own decision.

refs.note: Paul affirms that a woman is bound to her husband as long as he lives; after his death she is free to remarry.
The principle of not being ‘yoked together with unbelievers’ extends the requirement that the widow’s new husband ‘must belong to the Lord.’
Paul later advises younger widows to remarry — a context-dependent instruction that does not contradict the preference for singleness stated here.

notes.note: Whether the ‘man’ is a father or a fiancé, the principle is the same: marrying is not sinful, but not marrying is better in the present circumstances. Paul gives the engaged couple full freedom to marry while gently recommending that, if self-control is present and circumstances permit, remaining unmarried allows greater focus on the Lord’s work.

The widow is bound to her husband as long as he lives — a restatement of the marriage bond’s permanence. After his death, she is free to remarry, but only ‘in the Lord.’ This phrase establishes the principle of endogamy: believers should marry believers.

Calvin reads this as advice to the engaged man: if his passions are strong, he should marry without scruple. If he has the gift of self-control and no compelling reason to marry, remaining celibate is better. Calvin insists that both choices are honourable; the difference is one of degree, not kind.

Calvin emphasises that ‘only in the Lord’ is not advice but command: a Christian widow may remarry only a Christian man. The freedom to remarry is real; the constraint to marry within the faith is equally real.

The identity of the ‘man’ (tis) and his ‘virgin’ (parthenos) is the most debated interpretive question in the chapter. The NIV translates as ‘the virgin he is engaged to,’ taking the man as the fiancé.
Paul’s closing ‘I think that I too have the Spirit of God’ (dokō de kagō pneuma theou echein) is likely litotes (understatement): Paul is confident he speaks by the Spirit, not merely guessing.

Fee favours the interpretation that vv. 36–38 address a father’s decision about his daughter’s marriage, based on the cultural context of patria potestas. However, he acknowledges that the personal language of v. 37 (‘he has settled the matter in his own mind’) makes the betrothed-couple interpretation compelling.

Fee reads Paul’s closing remark as a gentle assertion of Spirit-inspired authority: those who claim the Spirit (‘I too have the Spirit of God’) should recognise that Paul’s judgment is not merely human opinion but Spirit-guided apostolic counsel.

Thiselton surveys all three interpretations (father, fiancé, spiritual marriage) and concludes that the betrothed-couple reading best fits the Greek syntax, the letter’s context, and the parallel structure with vv. 25–28.

Thiselton notes that ‘only in the Lord’ (monon en kyriō) functions as a christological constraint on freedom: the widow’s liberty to remarry is genuine but bounded by her primary allegiance to Christ.

hist.context: Paul concludes the chapter with two specific cases: the engaged couple (vv. 36–38) and the widow (vv. 39–40). Both receive the same nuanced counsel: marriage is fully acceptable, but remaining single is preferable given the eschatological circumstances. The widow is ‘free to marry anyone she wishes, but he must belong to the Lord’ (v. 39) — the earliest explicit NT instruction that remarriage must be to a believer. Paul closes by expressing his opinion that the widow is ‘happier’ if she stays as she is, and adds ‘I think that I too have the Spirit of God’ (v. 40) — a statement that is either gently ironic (understatement) or a defence of his apostolic authority against those who questioned it.

themes.note: Sovereignty and Holiness dominate: God assigns each person’s calling, and the body’s sexual conduct falls under divine lordship. Covenant rises through the marriage-bond language and the ‘only in the Lord’ constraint. Mission scores moderately: Paul’s concern for ‘undivided devotion’ is ultimately missional.

rows.text: Marriage, Conjugal Rights, Divorce, and Mixed Marriages

rows.label: vv.17–24
rows.text: Remain in Your Calling: Circumcision and Slavery

rows.label: vv.25–35
rows.text: The Present Crisis and Eschatological Detachment

lit.note: Chapter 7 is Paul’s most sustained teaching on marriage, structured by a governing principle (‘remain as you were called,’ vv. 17, 20, 24) and shaped by eschatological urgency (‘the time is short,’ v. 29). Paul navigates between ascetic denial and libertine indulgence, affirming both marriage and singleness as divine charismata while commending celibacy as strategically advantageous in the present eschatological moment.

hebtext.note: The noun charisma (gift) in v. 7 applies the language of spiritual gifts to marital status. Both marriage and celibacy are charismata — grace-gifts from God, not achievements or obligations. This framing prevents both the elevation of celibacy as inherently superior (an ascetic tendency) and the dismissal of singleness as deficient (a social convention). Paul thus relativises marital status: each person has received their own charisma from God.

The noun klēsis (calling) in vv. 17–24 undergoes a distinctive semantic shift. In standard Pauline usage, klēsis refers to God’s saving call (cf. 1:26; Rom 11:29). Here Paul extends the concept: the social situation in which a person was called (married/unmarried, circumcised/uncircumcised, slave/free) becomes itself a legitimate sphere for living out the divine call. Klēsis thus bridges soteriology and social ethics: the circumstances of conversion are not obstacles to faithfulness but arenas for it.

The adjective amerimnos (free from anxiety) in v. 32 captures Paul’s primary pastoral motive for commending singleness. The unmarried person is able to be amerimnos — undistracted in devotion to the Lord. Paul is not opposing marriage but acknowledging that marriage inevitably introduces divided attention (merimnaō, ‘to be anxious/concerned,’ vv. 32–34). His goal is the Corinthians’ benefit (to hymōn autōn symphoron, v. 35), not to impose restrictions.

The verb gamizō (to give in marriage) in vv. 36–38 is interpreted in three main ways: (1) a father deciding whether to give his virgin daughter in marriage; (2) a man deciding whether to marry his betrothed; (3) a ‘spiritual marriage’ (virgines subintroductae) where couples lived together in celibate partnership. Most modern scholars favour option (2), reading the passage as advice to a man about whether to marry his fiancée. The cultural context of arranged marriage in the Greco-Roman world makes option (1) plausible, but the personal language of vv. 36–37 suggests the man’s own decision.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: The principle is not a blanket endorsement of the status quo but a theological statement that salvation in Christ does not require social revolution as a precondition for faithful living…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Marriage, Singleness, and the Present Crisis

translations.text: The wife does not have authority over her own body but yields it to her husband. In the same way, the husband does not have authority over his own body but yields it to his wife.
For the wife does not have authority over her own body, but the husband does. Likewise the husband does not have authority over his own body, but the wife does.
But since sexual immorality is occurring, each man should have sexual relations with his own wife, and each woman with her own husband.
But because of the temptation to sexual immorality, each man should have his own wife and each woman her own husband.
